question_text: A student investigates how sound intensity varies with distance from a loudspeaker. At 1 metre from the speaker, the sound intensity is 0.1 W/m². At 2 metres, the intensity is 0.025 W/m². At 4 metres, the intensity is 0.00625 W/m². Identify the mathematical relationship between distance and intensity, and explain why this relationship exists for sound waves spreading in open air.
correct_answer: Intensity is inversely proportional to the square of distance (I ∝ 1/r²). This is the inverse square law. It occurs because sound energy spreads uniformly over an increasingly larger spherical surface as it radiates outward from a point source. Since the surface area of a sphere increases with the square of the radius (A = 4πr²), the same acoustic power becomes distributed over a larger area at greater distances, causing intensity to decrease proportionally to 1/r².
explanation: Sound waves travel outward from a loudspeaker in all directions, forming an expanding sphere. The total energy stays the same, but it spreads over a bigger and bigger surface. When you double the distance, the surface area increases by a factor of 4 (2²), so the intensity becomes 1/4 as strong. This pattern is called the inverse square law and applies to any point source of sound or light in open space.
